messages.content:
YOUR ROLE: You are the partner running a pre-mortem on this market model for a
paying client. The client's own team has already read every number in the
ledger, including every sensitivity band. Your reputation rests on surfacing
what THEY would not list: risks that look benign on the surface but could kill
the venture, argued mechanistically, anchored in the model.

HARD REQUIREMENTS — a hypothesis that misses any of these will be rejected by
a validator before any human reads it:
1. targetNodes: real ledger node ids; perturbation ops only on FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes.
2. mechanism: at least 3 steps (trigger → propagation → model impact), EVERY
   step carrying refs — "ledger:<nodeId>", "digest:<group>#c<n>", or
   "profile:<field>". A step you cannot reference is a step you are guessing.
3. falsifier: the single concrete observation that would kill this risk. If you
   cannot name one, it is not a claim.
4. whyMissable: why a competent founder's own risk list omits this. "It's
   non-obvious" is not an argument; name the surface appearance that hides it.
5. likelihood: a probability with a basis ("evidence" | "base-rate" |
   "judgment") and a rationale. No naked numbers.
6. indicators: at least 2 observable early warnings, each with WHERE to watch
   (a named tender portal, an operator's capex call, a standards docket, a
   vendor's price list) and the reading that should trigger re-assessment.
7. mitigations: at least 2. Where the right move is to buy information, use
   type "information" and name the ledger node it de-risks (voiNodeId) — its
   value will be computed, not asserted.

QUOTAS (per lens): at least one hypothesis self-tagged expectedObviousness
"low", and at least one whose perturbation targets NONE of tamBase /
serviceableFactor / obtainableFactor — the tornado already covers those bands;
look past them.

MINE THE RESIDUALS: the highest-yield material is where sources disagree with
the curated value (the digests' "tensions"), where searches came back empty
(the digests' "absences" — an unquantified market is a risk, not a comfort),
where a derivation's crossCheck diverges from its primary method, and where a
precise-looking value carries maturity "needs-source".

HOW YOUR CLAIM BECOMES A NUMBER (the perturbation contract):
- You never state € impacts. You emit machine-readable ops; the engine computes
  ΔTAM/ΔSAM/ΔYAM by re-running the real funnel. Any € figure you write in prose
  will be stripped.
- Ops: {nodeId, op: "set"|"scale"|"add", value} rewrite one leaf's value;
  {nodeId, op: "exclude"} removes a dimension cell from the venture's reachable
  scope ("unavailable to us" — foreclosure), which is different from shrinking it.
- Ops apply in array order. Ratio nodes must stay inside [0,1]; tamBase must stay > 0.
- Only FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes may be perturbed (marked in the ledger you receive).
  shape.cagr and shape.cr3 are context — a demand-pace or concentration risk must
  be translated into the funnel nodes it would actually move (tamBase level,
  serviceableFactor reach, obtainableFactor pace, or a share).
- Sibling shares are NOT re-normalized when you move one share (documented v1
  simplification). If you mean a mix-SHIFT, perturb multiple siblings; if you
  mean "this slice is smaller in absolute terms", one op is correct.
- Perturbing an assumption leaf overrides whatever the user set: your claim is
  about the world, not about their slider.

BANNED GENRES (emitting one of these wastes your slot):
- "Find 2 customers to talk to and get an LOI" — an action item, not a risk. It names no node, no mechanism, no falsifier.
- "Do research on customer buying behaviour" — a homework assignment any consultant could write for any company.
- "Competition is strong / the market is concentrated" — the ledger already states CR3. Restating a fact is not a risk; the risk is a MECHANISM that concentration enables.
- "The market may be smaller than expected" — the tamBase band (240–520) already says this. Only worth saying if you name WHICH derivation hop fails and WHY.
- "Regulatory changes could impact the business" — name the instrument, the compliance artifact it requires, and the date it lands, or drop it.
- "The band's low end might be true" with no new reason — the band is already in the model; you are paid for what is NOT in the model.

GOLD EXEMPLAR (the target register — do NOT repeat its content):
{
 "id": "busway-hollowing-hyperscale",
 "title": "Busway + OCP power shelves hollow out the hyperscale 44% from inside the scope boundary",
 "narrative": "The model excludes busbars and power shelves as out-of-scope substitutes — a scoping footnote. But hyperscale is 44% of TAM, and it is precisely the segment whose new AI-density builds are moving power distribution from rack PDUs to overhead busway feeding OCP-style in-rack power shelves. If that architecture becomes the default for new CE hyperscale capacity, the largest slice of this TAM is not 'ours to win slowly' — it is structurally shrinking while the headline number stays flat. The exclusion list quietly became the market thesis.",
 "category": "boundary",
 "targetNodes": [
  "seg.hyperscale"
 ],
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "New hyperscale AI-density builds adopt busway + in-rack DC power shelves instead of discrete rack PDUs",
   "refs": [
    "digest:seg-mix#c?",
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "CE hyperscale capacity additions (the growth the 9% CAGR is made of) carry falling rack-PDU content per MW; replacement demand in legacy halls does not offset it",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:shape.cagr",
    "digest:tam-base#c?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "The hyperscale share of the rack-PDU market shrinks in absolute terms — seg.hyperscale 0.44 overstates the addressable slice for a new entrant's window",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "whyMissable": "Substitutes were explicitly considered and excluded — the boundary decision looks handled. The kill path is not 'we misjudged the boundary' but 'the boundary moved into the biggest segment while the TAM base year stayed put'.",
 "expectedObviousness": "low",
 "falsifier": "2025–26 CE hyperscale fit-out specs (or Schneider/Vertiv rack-power order mixes) showing intelligent rack PDUs holding share in new AI-density halls.",
 "likelihood": {
  "value": 0.35,
  "rationale": "OCP power-shelf adoption is documented for AI racks, but CE colocation and enterprise retrofits (56% of TAM) keep buying rack PDUs; timing within a 12-month window is uncertain.",
  "basis": "evidence"
 },
 "perturbation": [
  {
   "nodeId": "seg.hyperscale",
   "op": "set",
   "value": 0.22,
   "note": "≈half of the hyperscale slice migrates to busway/shelf architectures"
  }
 ],
 "indicators": [
  {
   "signal": "Rack-power architecture specified in new CE hyperscale tenders (busway/shelf vs PDU)",
   "where": "Frankfurt/Amsterdam data-center fit-out tenders; OCP regional summit deployment talks",
   "threshold": "≥2 flagship CE builds specifying shelf-based distribution",
   "updates": "increases"
  },
  {
   "signal": "Incumbent rack-PDU revenue mix commentary (Vertiv/Schneider earnings)",
   "where": "Quarterly earnings calls, rack-power product-line commentary",
   "threshold": "PDU line flat while busway line grows double-digit",
   "updates": "increases"
  }
 ],
 "mitigations": [
  {
   "action": "Commission a CE rack-power architecture survey across the 2026 hyperscale build pipeline",
   "type": "information",
   "voiNodeId": "seg.hyperscale"
  },
  {
   "action": "Qualify the product line for colocation/enterprise retrofit first, where PDU form-factor is sticky",
   "type": "strategic"
  }
 ]
}

content: {"hypotheses":[{"id":"ce-hyperscale-global-split-mirage","title":"CE hyperscale 0.44 is a global-split mirage: Europe's self-build sits in Ireland/Sweden/Belgium, and Frankfurt is colocation","narrative":"The segment axis applies Synergy's GLOBAL capacity split (48% hyperscale × 0.91) to a geography axis built entirely on Central European colocation MW. But CBRE's Europe-specific data says ~60% of operational hyperscaler self-build capacity sits in Ireland, the Netherlands, Sweden and Belgium — three of the four outside CE-7 — and that European colocation 'will remain significantly larger' than hyperscale self-build. Inside CE, hyperscalers show up mostly as TENANTS in Frankfurt and Amsterdam colo halls, where the fit-out and PDU procurement decision often sits with the colo operator or a global tenant framework, not a CE buyer. The model's biggest cell — DE × hyperscale — is therefore substantially phantom, and the true CE mix is inverted toward colocation buyers with different procurement paths.","category":"model-structure","targetNodes":["seg.hyperscale","seg.colocation"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"CBRE Q4-2025: ~60% of Europe's operational hyperscaler self-build capacity is located in Ireland, Netherlands, Sweden, Belgium; and colocation in Europe 'will remain significantly larger' than hyperscaler self-build — the opposite ordering from the model's 0.44 vs 0.18","refs":["digest:seg-mix#c16","digest:seg-mix#c15"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The 0.44 was produced by rescaling a raw GLOBAL Synergy share; no Europe- or CE-scoped hyperscale share has ever been observed, and the 'cross-check' (Q1-2025 44% global) is a different quantity that coincidentally matches. Hyperscale demand in CE flows through colo halls whose take-up already outstrips supply","refs":["ledger:seg.hyperscale","digest:seg-mix#c6","digest:seg-mix#c24"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The DE(0.50) × hyperscale(0.44) cell — the largest cell in the cube and the implied beachhead for selling to large operators — is largely phantom in CE; weight belongs in colocation, whose buyer, spec and procurement cadence differ from what the venture is building for","refs":["ledger:geo.DE","ledger:seg.colocation","profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?"]}],"whyMissable":"seg.hyperscale carries maturity 'triangulated' with multiple Synergy vintages agreeing on trajectory — it looks like the best-sourced segment node. The agreement is real but among GLOBAL figures; nothing in the ledger flags that the quantity was never observed at European scope, and Frankfurt's AI headlines make 'hyperscale dominates CE' feel confirmed daily.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"A Europe- or CE-scoped capacity split (CBRE, Synergy regional cut, or EUDCA country tables) showing hyperscale-owned capacity at ≥40% of CE-7 total data-center capacity.","likelihood":{"value":0.5,"rationale":"CBRE's Europe-specific reporting directly contradicts the global ordering, and the cross-corpus tension flags that no CE-scoped hyperscale share exists; the open question is only magnitude, not direction.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"seg.hyperscale","op":"set","value":0.28,"note":"CE hyperscale-owned share far below global 0.44; self-build concentrated outside CE-7"},{"nodeId":"seg.colocation","op":"set","value":0.32,"note":"Mix-shift: the displaced weight lands in colocation, per CBRE's European ordering"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Europe hyperscale self-build vs colocation GW split, updated quarterly","where":"CBRE 'Europe Data Centres' quarterly figures report; DCD coverage of CBRE releases","threshold":"CE-7 hyperscale self-build share reported <30% of CE capacity","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Country-level ownership mix (colocation/hyperscale/enterprise) for DE and NL","where":"EUDCA 'State of European Data Centres' annual report country tables","threshold":"Germany capacity shown as majority colocation-operated","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Commission a CE-scoped ownership split (hyperscale-owned vs colo vs enterprise MW for the CE-7) before locking segment-weighted GTM targets","type":"information","voiNodeId":"seg.hyperscale","note":"Replaces the rescaled global split with an observed regional quantity"},{"action":"Build the launch spec and reference designs around colocation operators and their fit-out integrators rather than hyperscaler direct accounts","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Aligns product qualification with the segment that actually dominates CE"}]},{"id":"global-framework-forecloses-large-operator-cell","title":"Global framework agreements foreclose the large-operator channel — the venture's declared first buyer — from a CE entry point","narrative":"The model gives large operators 0.40 of buyer spend, and the venture plans to sell to them first. But hyperscalers and pan-European colos (Equinix, Digital Realty — exactly the accounts on Vertiv's published major-customer list) qualify rack-PDU vendors on global framework agreements and approved-vendor lists negotiated at HQ on multi-year cycles. That spend is not addressable from a Central European regional sales motion in a 12-month window; it is not 'ours to win slowly', it is contractually and organizationally elsewhere. The 0.40 itself rests on a misread company-level order-mix statistic, not any channel data. The venture's GTM points at the one cell of the cube that is structurally closed to it.","category":"competitive","targetNodes":["cust.operator-large"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Large-operator PDU procurement runs through global AVLs and framework agreements: Vertiv's named major customers are the global hyperscale/colo/telecom accounts, and industrial suppliers report data-center demand concentrating in exactly these buyers","refs":["digest:cust-mix#c9","digest:cust-mix#c0"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The 0.40 large-operator share has no channel evidence behind it — its sole datapoint is one supplier's 30%-of-orders stat, and no source publishes any numeric buyer-channel split — so the model cannot distinguish 'large operators buy 40% of PDUs' from 'large operators buy 40% of PDUs via global frameworks a CE entrant cannot bid into'","refs":["ledger:cust.operator-large","digest:cust-mix#c0"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"For this venture's reachable scope, the large-operator cell is foreclosed rather than merely competitive; the declared first-buyer strategy targets it anyway, so SAM and Year-1 pipeline are built on a cell the funnel counts but the venture cannot access","refs":["profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?","ledger:cust.operator-large"]}],"whyMissable":"'Sell to large operators first' looks like the conservative, high-credibility move — big logos, big volumes. The buyer-mix node even carries a wide band, giving the impression the uncertainty is priced. What the band cannot express is reachability: the share may be exactly 0.40 and still be zero for a regional entrant, because the purchase decision physically happens in another region under an existing framework.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"Two or more CE-region large operators (hyperscale or pan-European colo) confirming country-level PDU sourcing authority and a documented case of a new vendor entering their AVL via a regional pilot within 12 months.","likelihood":{"value":0.45,"rationale":"Global AVL procurement at hyperscale/pan-European colo accounts is well documented qualitatively; uncertainty is whether CE country organizations retain any local sourcing discretion for retrofit and expansion projects.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"cust.operator-large","op":"exclude","value":null,"note":"Foreclosure, not shrinkage: the large-operator cell exists in the market but is unavailable to a CE regional entrant in the model's horizon"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Vendor-qualification and supplier-registration requirements for rack power at pan-European colos","where":"Equinix and Digital Realty supplier/procurement portals; framework tender notices on TED (tenders.europa.eu) for DC fit-outs","threshold":"PDU category listed only under global framework agreements with no regional qualification path","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Where incumbent PDU wins at large accounts are booked","where":"Vertiv and Schneider quarterly earnings calls, commentary on hyperscale framework awards","threshold":"Rack-power growth attributed predominantly to global framework expansions rather than regional project wins","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Run three structured CE operator/distributor procurement interviews specifically probing local vs HQ sourcing authority for rack PDUs (the node's own promotion path)","type":"information","voiNodeId":"cust.operator-large","note":"Directly tests reachability of the 0.40 cell before GTM spend commits to it"},{"action":"Open a parallel OEM/white-label route via rack integrators, which is documented as an existing channel and does not require AVL entry","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Digipower/GETEK-style OEM supply reaches large-operator racks without winning the framework"}]},{"id":"colo-denominator-mispricing-enterprise-geography","title":"Colo-MW geo weights misprice the enterprise third of the cube: NL inflated, Swiss and German enterprise demand invisible by construction","narrative":"Every geography share was apportioned on Statista's colocation-and-scale-colocation MW. But the segment axis says 29% of the market is enterprise on-premise — capacity that the colo denominator does not count at all. The error is not random: Switzerland's installed base including self-operated capacity is ~3x its colo figure, and Germany's installed IT load (BMWK >2,700 MW) far exceeds its 1,737 MW colo figure, while the Netherlands' weight reflects the Amsterdam colo hub, not Dutch enterprise demand. So the funnel multiplies an enterprise segment share by geo weights that systematically inflate NL×enterprise and starve CH×enterprise and DE×enterprise. Country sequencing, channel coverage and SAM built on these weights are wrong for a third of the market — and the mismatch is invisible because the summed shares still equal 1.0.","category":"model-structure","targetNodes":["geo.NL","geo.CH"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"All seven geo nodes are derived from a single colocation-scoped table whose country figures don't reconcile with other benchmarks (summed Statista colo exceeds CBRE's total European capacity), and no second vendor uses the same scope","refs":["digest:geo-region-denominator#c0","digest:geo-region-denominator#c1","digest:geo-region-denominator#c3"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The segment axis includes enterprise on-prem at 0.29, but enterprise capacity is exactly what colo MW excludes: Switzerland is self-operation-heavy (colo 274 MW vs ~850 MW installed base) and Germany's installed load exceeds its colo figure by ~1,000 MW — the geo weights are the wrong measure for the enterprise slice by construction","refs":["ledger:seg.enterprise","ledger:geo.CH","ledger:geo.DE"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"NL's 0.28 — a colo-hub weight — is applied to enterprise demand it doesn't represent, while CH's 0.08 understates a self-operation-heavy market; ~29% of TAM is misallocated across countries, distorting SAM reach and the country entry order","refs":["ledger:geo.NL","ledger:serviceableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"geo.NL is the greenest node in the geography axis — 'triangulated', Statista and the Dutch Data Center Association agree within 3%. But both sources are colocation-scoped, so the triangulation certifies the precision of the wrong denominator. A maturity flag that says 'well-sourced' hides a scope that says 'wrong measure for a third of the cube'.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"A total-installed-IT-load country apportionment for CE-7 (all segments) that reproduces the colo-based weights within a few points — i.e., NL ≈ 0.28 and CH ≈ 0.08 of total load, showing colo MW is a fair proxy for the full market.","likelihood":{"value":0.55,"rationale":"The ledger's own cross-checks already show CH diverging 0.08 vs 0.13 and DE 0.42 vs 0.53 depending on scope; that the divergence is systematic (colo hubs up, enterprise-heavy countries down) follows directly from the documented scope definitions.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"geo.NL","op":"set","value":0.23,"note":"Blended weight once the enterprise slice is apportioned on installed load instead of Amsterdam colo MW"},{"nodeId":"geo.CH","op":"set","value":0.12,"note":"Self-operated Swiss capacity re-enters the weighting for the enterprise slice"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"National installed-IT-load figures published alongside colo figures","where":"BMWK German data-centre landscape updates; CBRE Suisse commercial vs self-operated capacity reports","threshold":"Installed-load country shares diverging >5pp from the model's colo-based shares","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Statista vs CBRE scope reconciliation for 2025/2026 vintages","where":"CBRE Europe Data Centres quarterly vs Statista colocation-by-country table updates","threshold":"Gap between summed Statista country colo and CBRE European totals persisting or widening","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Build a dual-denominator geo split: colo MW weights for the hyperscale/colo cells, installed-IT-load weights for the enterprise cells, and recompute the cube","type":"information","voiNodeId":"geo.CH","note":"Cheap desk exercise with sources already in the ledger (BMWK, CBRE Suisse, Mordor installed-base)"},{"action":"Sequence country entry per segment rather than per blended geo weight — e.g., CH/DE for enterprise retrofit, NL for colo — instead of one country ranking for all segments","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Prevents the blended weights from steering channel investment into phantom cells"}]},{"id":"year1-obtainable-correlated-with-slowest-cell","title":"Year-1 obtainable is correlated with the slowest cell: large-operator qualification cycles push real Year-1 capture an order of magnitude below 3%","narrative":"The 3% obtainable factor comes from generic SOM benchmarks that assume Year-1 capture is drawn evenly from the SAM. But the venture has declared it will concentrate Year-1 effort on large operators — precisely the buyer cell with the longest procurement cycle: AVL qualification, certification review, pilot deployment, framework negotiation, typically longer than the entire 12-month horizon. The funnel treats obtainable pace and buyer mix as independent; the venture's own GTM choice makes them strongly negatively correlated. The hardware-entrant analogues the corpus surfaced (worked examples at ~0.1% of SAM, real entrants starting near 0.001%) all point the same direction: a pre-launch entrant selling into qualification-gated accounts books close to nothing in year one, whatever the market size says.","category":"execution","targetNodes":["obtainableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"The declared GTM concentrates Year-1 pipeline in large operators at pre-launch stage — accounts held by incumbents with named global relationships (Vertiv's customer list) and formal vendor-qualification gates","refs":["profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?","profile:Stage","digest:cust-mix#c9"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The 3% is anchored on generic early-stage SOM benchmarks (1–5%) with no DC-hardware specificity, and its own cross-check history shows the prior 6% was already revised down once; nothing in the benchmark reflects qualification-cycle length in the chosen buyer cell","refs":["ledger:obtainableFactor","ledger:cust.operator-large"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"Obtainable pace is a function of the buyer cell it is applied to; concentrated on the slowest cell, the effective Year-1 factor sits far below the 0.01 low band edge, and YAM overstates first-year revenue by roughly an order of magnitude","refs":["ledger:obtainableFactor","digest:cust-mix#c0"]}],"whyMissable":"'Conservative ramp' was explicitly selected and the factor was already revised down from 6% to 3% — the box looks double-checked. What the revision didn't touch is the correlation: the same profile that set the pace assumption also set the buyer target, and the two choices interact multiplicatively in a funnel that models them as independent sliders.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"A documented case of a new rack-PDU entrant (or comparable DC hardware entrant) closing ≥3% of a regional SAM within 12 months selling direct to large operators — or the venture's own first RFQ-to-PO completing in under 6 months at a large account.","likelihood":{"value":0.6,"rationale":"Multiple independent benchmark critiques in the corpus (multi-year SOM windows, hardware entrants at 0.001–0.1% of SAM) plus documented incumbent lock at large accounts all point below the 1% band floor for this specific buyer strategy.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"obtainableFactor","op":"set","value":0.008,"note":"Year-1 capture when pipeline is concentrated in qualification-gated large accounts; below the model's 0.01 band floor"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Elapsed time and stage-gates in the venture's first large-operator qualification processes","where":"Own CRM pipeline plus operator supplier-registration portals (Equinix/DLR vendor onboarding)","threshold":"First qualification not complete within 6 months of launch","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"DC fit-out and framework tender award cycles for rack power in CE","where":"TED (tenders.europa.eu) award notices vs publication dates for data-centre electrical packages","threshold":"Median publication-to-award >9 months","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Interview three CE operators on PDU vendor-qualification timelines and pilot-to-PO conversion, and rebuild obtainableFactor as win-rate × cycle-time × capacity per buyer cell","type":"information","voiNodeId":"obtainableFactor","note":"The node's own promotion path, executed before launch rather than after"},{"action":"Route Year-1 revenue through distributor and OEM cells in parallel so first-year cash is decorrelated from large-account qualification cycles","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Master-distributor and white-label channels are documented and faster-cycle"}]},{"id":"enterprise-share-is-stock-not-spend","title":"Enterprise 0.29 is a capacity stock, not PDU spend: a declining, refresh-lagged slice inflates nearly a third of TAM","narrative":"seg.enterprise carries 29% of the market because Synergy says enterprise on-prem holds 32% of global CAPACITY. But rack-PDU revenue follows flows — new builds and refresh — not standing stock. Enterprise on-prem is the one segment in structural decline (56% in 2018 → 32% now → 19% by 2031, minus two points a year): near-zero new-build demand, refresh cycles stretched as workloads migrate out, and the node's own provenance admits refresh demand 'may lag capacity share'. Meanwhile the geo axis never measured enterprise at all (colo denominator), so this slice is doubly unobserved. A third of the TAM is priced off the one number — stock share — that is least predictive of what an entrant can invoice.","category":"fact","targetNodes":["seg.enterprise"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Enterprise on-prem capacity share is in documented structural decline: 56% (2018) → 32% (end-2025) → 19% (2031), falling ~2pp per year","refs":["digest:seg-mix#c2","digest:seg-mix#c5","digest:seg-mix#c22"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"PDU purchases track capacity flows, not stock: a shrinking installed base generates minimal new-build PDU demand and deferred refresh, so spend share sits well below capacity share — a lag the node's own rationale flags but does not quantify; the GenAI on-prem boost is described only as relief 'after a sustained period of essentially no growth'","refs":["ledger:seg.enterprise","digest:seg-mix#c20"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"seg.enterprise 0.29 overstates the invoiceable enterprise slice of CE rack-PDU demand, and because the geo weights are colo-scoped this overstatement was never exposed to any country-level evidence — the funnel books phantom demand across every geo × enterprise cell","refs":["ledger:seg.enterprise","digest:geo-region-denominator#c0"]}],"whyMissable":"The 0.29 is arithmetically clean — Synergy's 32% × the 0.91 carve-out — and the declining trend is right there in the cited excerpt, which makes it feel priced in. It isn't: the model uses the current-year stock share as a spend share, and the decline is exactly why stock overstates spend. The number being visibly sourced hides that it measures the wrong quantity.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"Vendor rack-PDU revenue mix (Schneider/Vertiv/Eaton product-line or channel commentary, or an Omdia/IndexBox application split) showing enterprise/on-prem channel revenue at or above ~29% of rack-PDU sales — i.e., spend share tracking capacity share.","likelihood":{"value":0.45,"rationale":"The stock-vs-flow mismatch is structural and the decline is multi-vintage-consistent, but the GenAI/GPU on-prem build wave (Synergy's own caveat) could temporarily lift enterprise PDU spend above its stock-share trend.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"seg.enterprise","op":"set","value":0.18,"note":"Flow-weighted enterprise share of PDU spend for a declining stock at -2pp/yr with stretched refresh; absolute shrinkage, not a mix-shift, so siblings untouched"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Enterprise/on-prem share in successive Synergy vintages and any GenAI-driven on-prem build commentary","where":"Synergy Research quarterly capacity-share releases; CRN coverage of the hyperscale-vs-enterprise split","threshold":"On-prem share falling faster than 2pp/yr, or GenAI boost fading from the narrative","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Channel mix for rack power sold into enterprise vs DC operators","where":"Schneider/Eaton/Vertiv earnings-call commentary and distributor (IT-VAR) rack-power sell-through reports","threshold":"Enterprise/IT-channel rack-PDU revenue flat or declining YoY while operator segment grows double-digit","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Obtain one application-split datapoint for rack-PDU revenue by end-user (IndexBox EU report application tables or Omdia vendor/application split) to replace the capacity-as-spend assumption","type":"information","voiNodeId":"seg.enterprise","note":"A single sourced spend-share resolves the stock-vs-flow gap for a third of TAM"},{"action":"Treat enterprise as a refresh-only opportunity in planning: qualify via IT distributors for retrofit, and exclude enterprise new-build from Year-1 pipeline targets","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Aligns sales capacity with the demand that actually flows from a declining stock"}]}]}
